steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Which type of radiation is used in smoke detectors?
options: Gamma, because it can penetrate the detector casing
Beta, because it is stopped by the aluminium housing
Alpha, because it ionises the air inside the detector
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Smoke detectors use alpha radiation (specifically from americium-241). Alpha ionises the air between two electrodes, creating a small current. Smoke particles absorb the alpha and reduce this current, triggering the alarm.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Alpha radiation ionises the air in a smoke detector. When smoke is present it absorbs the alpha, the ionisation drops, and the alarm sounds.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.4.3 — Uses and hazards of nuclear radiation

steps.title: Uses of Radiation
steps.bodyHTML: Radioactive isotopes have important medical uses: gamma-emitting tracers (e.g. technetium-99m) are used to image organs, and high-energy gamma beams are used in radiotherapy to destroy cancer cells. In industry, beta radiation is used in paper and metal thickness gauges, gamma sterilises medical equipment and food, and alpha is used in smoke detectors. The choice of isotope depends on its radiation type, half-life, and the risks and benefits for each application.
commonErrors: Thinking any type of radiation can be used for any application — the type must be matched carefully
Forgetting that the half-life must be appropriate (short for tracers, longer for industrial sources)
Assuming radiation is only harmful and has no beneficial uses

aqa.questionText: A hospital uses a radioactive tracer to image a patient’s thyroid gland. Explain why a gamma-emitting isotope with a short half-life is chosen, rather than an alpha-emitting isotope with a long half-life.

steps.label: Explain why gamma is chosen
steps.content: Gamma radiation can pass through body tissue and be detected outside the body. Alpha radiation is stopped by a few centimetres of tissue and could not be detected externally.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — gamma penetrates tissue and can be detected outside the body

steps.label: Explain why a short half-life is chosen
steps.content: A short half-life means the activity falls quickly, reducing the patient’s total radiation dose after the scan is complete.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — short half-life reduces radiation dose to patient

steps.label: Explain why alpha with a long half-life is unsuitable
steps.content: Alpha is highly ionising and would cause significant tissue damage inside the body, and a long half-life means the patient remains radioactive for a long time.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — alpha causes more damage inside the body / long half-life increases dose

aqa.finalAnswer: Gamma penetrates tissue; short half-life minimises patient dose. Alpha would cause internal damage and remain in body too long.

aqa.questionText: A factory uses a radioactive source to monitor the thickness of aluminium sheets. Which type of radiation should be used?

options.label: A
options.text: Alpha — it is stopped by a sheet of paper so any aluminium would absorb it entirely

options.label: B
options.text: Beta — it is partially absorbed by aluminium, so the count rate varies with thickness

options.label: C
options.text: Gamma — it passes straight through aluminium without any absorption

options.label: D
options.text: Any type can be used as they all travel the same distance through aluminium

markSchemeSteps: Beta is partially absorbed by aluminium — if the sheet is too thick, count rate drops; if too thin, count rate rises ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Gamma passes through aluminium with very little absorption, so it cannot detect small changes in thickness.
